variationDimensions.title: COUNTING VARIATIONS
variationDimensions.text: When practicing a technique we can vary the number of breaths we take. For example; in box breathing, we can change and increase from 4:4:4:4 to 6:6:6:6 or 8:8:8:8 or in cleansing and clearing breath we could increase from 1 x 30 / 1 x 60 / 1 x 90.

variationDimensions.title: TOUCH VARIATIONS
variationDimensions.text: When practicing with touch variation we can use our hands to create different breath focus. For example in 3-part breath we can practice, both hands on belly, one hand of belly and one of chest and then finally, hands rest in the lap (no touch.)

variationDimensions.title: NUMBER OF ROUNDS VARIATIONS
variationDimensions.text: When practicing with rounds variation we can vary the quantity of repetitive amount of cycles per technique execution. For example in cleansing & clearing breath we could change and increase each round from 1 x 30 / 3 x 30 / 5 x 30.

variationDimensions.title: THE GAZE VARIATIONS
variationDimensions.text: When practicing with the variations of the gaze, we are speaking about where the eyes are or how we place our vision. For example in the even breath, we can give variation by practicing; eyes open, narrowed gaze, eyes closed.

variationDimensions.title: SPEED & PACE VARIATIONS
variationDimensions.text: When practicing with speed & pace we can change the timing and space between the breaths. For example in cleansing & clearing breathe we could change the pace from 1 x 30 slow / 1 x 30 moderate / 1 x 30 fast.

variationDimensions.title: ENERGY & EFFORT VARIATIONS
variationDimensions.text: When practicing with energy and effort variations we can adapt the heat and enthusiasm within the chosen technique. For example with the extended exhale breath your could offer options of gentle exhale, intentional clearing, forced clearing.

variationDimensions.title: NOSE & MOUTH VARIATIONS
variationDimensions.text: When practicing with nose & mouth variation we have the option of directing or redirecting the air flow. For example, in the interrupted inhale we can vary the extended exhale from mouth closed, to mouth open to a sign.

variationDimensions.title: SUPPORT VARIATIONS
variationDimensions.text: When practicing with support variations we are offering supportive or held variations. For example in alternate nostril breathing you practice; standard offering, non-used hand tucked across the body in the arm pit, and finally both hands tucked across the body (with no hands on nose and practicing subtly.)

variationDimensions.title: VOLUME VARIATIONS
variationDimensions.text: When practicing with volume variation we can alter the sound or the breath in audible techniques. For example in the humming breath, we could vary at volumes of quiet, moderate to loud.

variationDimensions.title: SEAT POSITIONS VARIATIONS
variationDimensions.text: When practicing with a variation in your seat, is to simply change your seat position. In many practices you could offer from; seated, reclined or rested on the floor as a practice progression.

techniques.title: Energising breath
techniques.text: This practice invites the participant to constrict the epiglottis in order to wash the breath across the back of the throat, helping to regulate the breath, warms and energise the body and calm the mind.
howTo: Hold your palm in front of your mouth and breath air out in the same way that you would if you were fogging up a mirror, creating a gentle washing sound. Notice the activation at the back of your throat
Keep your hand where it is and inhale making the same sound
Practice for a few rounds
Now, begin to use this same activation of the throat but seal your lips on the exhale and breath out through your nose
Practice for a few rounds
Now, close your mouth and breath in and out through the nose so that the sound of the breath is the same on the inhale and exhale
Practice for one minute and release

techniques.title: Cleansing and clearing
techniques.text: A breathing technique in which the exhalation is active and the inhalation is passive. Creating heat in the body and strengthening the abdomen, as well as aiding digestion and clearing the nadis.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Bring your awareness to you lower belly
Take a long breath in through your nose
Contract the muscles in the abdomen in quick but regulated succession to force out short exhalations; the inhalation will be a passive reaction to your active exhalation
Continue this active exhalation for one minute
Inhale deeply through the nostrils and exhale slowly through the mouth

techniques.title: Bellows breath
techniques.text: A breathing technique in which both the inhalation and exhalation come from active contractions of the abdominals and diaphragm. Helping to strengthen the abdomen, drain excess mucus from the lungs, and energise both the body and mind.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Take a long breath in and out through the nose
Take another long breath in and exhale by quickly contracting the abdominal muscles
Follow this with a quick diaphragmatic inhale so that the breath moves into and out of the lungs in quick succession
Continue for one minute
When you have finished take a long slow breath in through the nose and out through the mouth

techniques.title: 3-Part interrupted inhale
techniques.text: Brief pauses are taken during the inhale. The practice introduces the concept of expanding the length and capacity of the inhalation.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat or reclined position
Take a few clearing breaths
On the next inhale fill the lungs to just one third of their capacity
Take a short pause
Fill the next third
Take a short pause
Fill the final third, or until the lungs are full with air
Take a final short pause
Exhale fully

techniques.title: Lion's breath
techniques.text: Lion's Breath is a technique to clear stale air in the body and stimulate the senses. Used in practice to immediate de-stimulate and reduce stress in the body.
howTo: Lean the upper body forward slightly
Inhale through your nose with your mouth closed.
Open your mouth wide and stick out your tongue as much as you can, curling it down toward your chin.
Exhale forcefully with a “ha” sound, feeling the breath come from deep within and passing across your entire tongue.
Repeat 5-10 times

techniques.title: Circular nostril breathing (warming and energising)
techniques.text: Breathing practice in which we inhale only through the right nostril and exhale through the left. Encourages flow of breath through the respiratory system creating heat and energy within the body. Strengthens energy, willpower and enthusiasm.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Lift your right hand to the nose
Use your ring finger to close off your left nostril and breath in through your right nostril
Close the right nostril with your thumb, releasing left nostril and exhale through the left nostril
Continue in this way, inhaling through the right nostril and exhaling through the left for 1-3 minutes

techniques.title: Opposite alternate nostril breathing (inhale)
techniques.text: A breathing technique in which the participant inhales through alternating nostrils whilst exhaling through both. Helps to calm and focus the mind, as well as an aid to balance emotions.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Lift your right hand to the nose
Use your thumb to seal off your right nostril and breath in through your left nostril
Release the seal and exhale through both nostrils
Seal your left nostril using your ring finger and inhale through your right nostril
Release the seal and exhale again through both
This is one round, continue for 10 rounds

techniques.title: Elephant breath
techniques.text: This breathing practice is a dynamic and invigorating technique to stimulate and energise the body. This technique helps to increases oxygen levels in the bloodstream, temporarily stimulating the sympathetic nervous system, circulating more blood flow, and gently heating the physical body.
howTo: Stand with your feet shoulder width apart, knees slightly bent
Inhale quickly through your nose as you swing your arms forward and up overhead
Exhale quickly through your nose as you bend your knees and fold forward, keep your arms heavy
Repeat for a few rounds
Pause in stillness and come back to your natural breath

techniques.title: Breath of joy
techniques.text: Breath of Joy is a dynamic breathing technique designed to invigorate the body and uplift the sensation response in the body. In this practice, deep inhalations are accompanied by vigorous arm movements, followed by a forceful exhalation through the mouth. This rhythmic breathing pattern floods the body with oxygen, awakening the senses and energizing the mind. It releases tension, enhances circulation, and promotes mental clarity.
howTo: Stand with your feet shoulder width apart, knees slightly bent
Inhale one third of the breath through your nose as you swing your arms forward and up overhead
Inhale the next third of the breath through your nose as you swing your arms out to the side
Inhale to full capacity through your nose as you swing your arms forward and up overhead
Take a big exhale through your mouth to the sound of 'ha' as you bend your knees and fold forward, arms move back
Repeat for a few rounds

techniques.title: Horse stance breathing
techniques.text: Stand with the legs spread and the knees bent. Place one hand resting on each knee while leaning forward allowing the abdomen to drop/hang forward. This body position allows easy access to the abdomen engagement without restriction. This position is best used cleansing and clearing breaths and other energising breathing practices.
howTo: Stand with legs spread and knees bent
Place one hand resting on each knee while leaning forward
Allow the abdomen to drop/hang forward
Utilise a cleansing and clearing breath (P83) you can also try this with other energising breathing practices

techniques.title: Physiological sigh
techniques.text: The physiological sigh, or often called the cyclic sigh (when done in succession) is simple and effective down regulating, stress response breath.
howTo: Start by noticing the current state your breath quality
Take a full inhale through the nose
Take second inhale through the nose, at a lower capacity but filling the intake to maximum capacity
Pause at the top
Open the mouth and sigh with an extended exhale
Repeat 5-10 times

techniques.title: Extended exhale
techniques.text: This breathing pattern is a release technique to help quickly down regulate the nervous system and activate the parasympathetic nervous system. It is a slow controlled technique where the exhale is longer than the inhale and no breath-holding in between. This technique is helps with relaxation and tension decompression.
howTo: Breathe slowly to an comfortably full level
No pause needed
Exhale long, slow and deep with the mouth open and an audible sigh
Repeat as needed

techniques.title: Triangle breathing
techniques.text: A three part technique that helps slow down the breathing cycle and down regulate the nervous system. Triangle breathing focuses evenly on the inhale, pause at the top of the breath and the exhale. This pattern is known to help with stress and anxiousness.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Notice the current pace of the breath, regardless the state
Take a conscious breath to begin
Breathe in slowly for the count of 3 seconds or slow counting
Pause at the top of the inhale for 3 seconds or slow counting
Exhale slowly for the count of 3 seconds or slow counting
Repeat this for 10-12 rounds or 2 minutes
If a 3 count patterns feels too short/long or not supportive, increase or decrease to a count that creates calm and ease
Once completed, release the technique and return to a natural breathing pattern

techniques.title: 3 Part breathing
techniques.text: A breathing technique for deep breathing that increasing oxygen intake and a full body sense of calmness. This technique helps to create both internal movement of the breath while a sense of warming within the body. Belly/chest breathing can be used effectively to calm the nerves or awaken the senses.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Notice the current pace of the breath, regardless the state
Take a conscious breath to begin
Place one hand on the belly and one hand on the chest
Breathe slowly and fully into both belly and chest equally, feeling both hands rise and expand
Pause at the top of the breath for 1 second
Exhale slowly letting the belly and chest empty
Repeat this for 10 rounds or 60 seconds

techniques.title: 4 - 7- 8 Breathing
techniques.text:
Triangle Breathing Variation
This technique is an inverted variation of the triangle breathing technique, it was developed by an internationally renowned doctor and integrative medicine pioneer,
Dr. Andrew Weil, and according to Weil, it is the most effective way of managing anxiety and quelling errant stress responses. These breathing ratio is useful for combating anxiety because they help fortify the connection between mind and body. As you slow your breathing
and focus fully on the breaths, you're taking, and your parasympathetic nervous system becomes engaged.
howTo: Use the triangle breathing technique above as the foundation
Inhale for the count of 4 seconds or slow counting
Pause and hold the breath for the count of 7 seconds or slow counting
Exhale for the count of 8 seconds or slow counting
Repeat this for 10-12 rounds or 2 minutes

techniques.title: Pinhole breath
techniques.text: The pinhole breath is a technique that is effectively used to slow down the exhalation, down regulate the nervous system and reduce real time stress.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Notice the breath without changing it
Take a full inhale through the nose
Part your lips slightly
Exhale out slowly through your lips like exhaling through a small pinhole
Pause at the end of the breath
Repeat 10 times or 60 seconds

techniques.title: Low frequency humming breath
techniques.text: A gentle humming down regulation technique. The vibrations from this breathing technique are known to soothe the nervous system and to free the mind of agitation.
howTo: Gently place the thumbs over each tragus, applying gentle pressure
Allow the rest of your fingers to rest naturally along your forehead
With mouth closed take a slow breath in to full capacity
As you exhale slowly create a gentle humming sound
Repeat as needed

techniques.title: 3-Part interrupted exhale
techniques.text: Brief pauses are taken during the exhale. Introduces concept of lengthening the exhale to understand the true capacity of the lungs.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat or reclined position
Take a few clearing breaths
Take a full inhale filling the lungs to a comfortable capacity
Exhale one third of the breath
Take a short pause
Exhale the next third
Take a short pause
Exhale the final third, or until the lungs are empty of air
Take a final short pause
Inhale fully

techniques.title: Circular nostril breathing (cooling & calming)
techniques.text: Breathing practice in which we inhale only through the left nostril and exhale through the right. Encourages flow of breath through the respiratory system creating a cooling energy within the body and calming energy for the mind.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Lift your right hand to the nose
Use your thumb to close off your right nostril and breath in through your left nostril
Close the left nostril with your ring finger, releasing right nostril and exhale through the right nostril
Continue in this way, inhaling through the left nostril and exhaling through the right for 1-3 minutes

techniques.title: Cooling breath (variation 1)
techniques.text: This breathing practice requires the participant to be able to curl their tongue like a straw in order to draw the breath across the tongue. This cools the body, improves focus and reduces agitation, anger and anxiety.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Take a few neutral breaths
With mouth open curl the tongue length ways to create a straw shape
Gently inhale washing the breath across the tongue
At the end of the inhalation relax the tongue and close the mouth
Exhale through your nose
Repeat for 10-12 rounds noticing the cooling effect on the body

techniques.title: Cooling breath (variation 2)
techniques.text: This is another cooling breathing practice for those who are not able to curl their tongue (don't worry, it's genetic!) Rather than curling the tongue, the tongue is instead pressed against the back of the top teeth.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Take a few neutral breaths
Bring the teeth together, keeping the lips open and pressing the tip of the tongue to the back of the top teeth
Gently inhale, creating a soft hissing sound as the air moves in through the gaps in the teeth
At the end of inhalation release the teeth and tongue and close the lips
Exhale through your nose
Repeat 10-12 times, noticing the cooling effect on the body

techniques.title: Opposite alternate nostril breathing (exhale)
techniques.text: A breathing technique in which the participant inhales through both nostrils, and then alternates the exhale through left and right. Helps to promote patience, focus and control as well as relieving stress and anxiety.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Lift your right hand to the nose
Take an inhale through both nostrils
Use the ring finger to close off the left nostril and exhale only through the right
Release the seal and inhale again through both nostrils
Now close the right nostril with your thumb and exhale only through your left
This is one round, continue for 10 rounds

techniques.title: Box breathing
techniques.text: A breathing technique in which all four parts of the breath (inhalation, internal retention, exhalation, external retention) are all of equal length. Reduces physical stress symptoms and increased mental clarity.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Notice the current pace of the breath,regardless the state
Take a conscious breath to begin
Breathe in slowly for the count of 4 seconds or slow counting
Hold the breath at the top of the inhale for the count of 4
Exhale slowly to the count of 4
Repeat this for 30 rounds or 2 minutes
If a 4 count patterns feels too short, long or not supportive increase or decrease to a count that creates calm and ease
Once completed release the technique and return to a natural breathing pattern

techniques.title: Inspired pause
techniques.text: This breathing technique focuses on slowing the oxygen intake and exhalation. Too often when we breath we think value comes only from what we are doing, when there is significant value in moments when we are not breathing in or out. A significant and intentional pause aims to create a sense of non-physical ease, slow the heart rate down and expand the lung capacity.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Take a conscious breath to begin
Breathe in slowly filling the breath to a comfortable and manageable level
Without force, pause the breath without distinct muscular engagement
Hold for the count of 2 seconds or slow counting
Exhale gently
Increase/decrease pause/hold count as preferred

techniques.title: Alternative nostril breathing
techniques.text: Breath practice in which each nostril is alternately used for inhalation and exhalation. Calms nervous system, balances masculine and feminine energy, balances left and right hemisphere of the brain, fosters mental clarity and cultivates ability to concentrate.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Take your right hand and lift up to your nose
Cover your right nostril with your thumb and take a long slow inhale through your left nostril
Use your right ring finger to close off your left nostril at the same time releasing the right
Exhale through your right nostril, pause briefly at the end of the exhale, then inhale again through the right nostril
Close off your right nostril, open your left and exhale through the left
This is one round, complete for several rounds of breath

techniques.title: 3-Part interrupted inhale & exhale
techniques.text: Brief pauses are taken during the inhale and exhale. This technique helps slow down the breath and helps create a sense of calm and ease.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat or reclined position
Take a few clearing breaths
On the next inhale, fill the lungs to just one third of their capacity
Take a short pause
Fill the next third
Take a short pause
Fill the final third, or until the lungs are full with air
Take a short pause at the top
Exhale one third of the breath
Take a short pause
Exhale the next third
Take a short pause
Exhale the final third, or until the lungs are empty of air
Take a final short pause
Repeat for a few rounds

techniques.title: Conscious breath
techniques.text: A conscious breath is a slow considered breath to bring awareness into the body. A conscious breath is not dictated by quantity, but rather of a quality inhale and exhale to signal a focus and intention of the moment. Use this breath when beginning a practice or as a way to centre or reset into a state of complete awareness in the moment.
howTo: Sitting or standing, bring your attention completely onto the breath
Take a conscious breath in (does not need to be deep)
Exhale the breath out at ease (mouth open or close)
Repeat as needed
Repeat this for 10 rounds or 60 seconds

techniques.title: Recovery breath
techniques.text: This technique is a variation of the conscious breath used intentionally after a breathing round. The recovery breath, or return to natural breath is to ensure that when a practice round completes, we are not simply stopping, but rather, transitioning ourselves intentionally back to a natural breath.
howTo: At the completion of the last breath in the round, take a full inhale in
Hold the breath and pause to acknowledge the transition
Exhale fully with an extended breath helping to initiate a down regulation
The exhale can be mouth closed or open
Allow breathing pattern to return to a natural breath pace and flow

techniques.title: Diaphragmatic breathing
techniques.text:
Diaphragmatic breathing, also known as belly breathing is a deep breathing exercise that fully engages the diaphragm and increases the efficiency of the lungs.
This is a foundational technique for helping to regulate the nervous system and create a parasympathetic response. Belly breathing can be used pro actively to create a calming effect, and as a response in times of heightened stress or anxiousness.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat or symmetrical standing position
Notice the current pace of the breath, regardless the state
Take a conscious breath to begin
Place one hand on the belly, and one hand on the chest
Breathe slowly and fully into the belly, letting it expand
Pause at the top of the breath for 1 second
Exhale slowly letting the belly the empty
Repeat this for 10 rounds or 60 seconds

techniques.title: Costal breath or chest breathing
techniques.text: Costal breathing is a mode of breathing that requires contraction of the intercostal muscles. As the intercostal muscles relax, air passively leaves the lungs. This type of breathing is also known as shallow breathing.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat or symmetrical standing position
Notice the current pace of the breath, regardless the state
Take a conscious breath to begin
Breathe slowly into the chest feeling the expansion of the intercostal muscles
Exhale slowly letting the chest empty
Repeat to 10 rounds or 60 seconds

techniques.title: Clavicular breath or upper chest breathing
techniques.text: Clavicular breathing relies on the muscles of the chest, shoulders, upper back, and neck to elevate the upper rib cage and chest for each inhalation. This causes the upper chest and lungs to expand, resulting in inhalation.
howTo: Start by noticing the natural state of the breath
Breathe into the upper chest and lower throat space
Feel an 360 expansion, rather than an up and down action
Repeat to 10 rounds or 60 seconds

techniques.title: Even breath
techniques.text:
The steady inhalation and exhalation of equal duration.
A straightforward practice that helps to soothe both body and mind. A technique to help slow down the breathing cycle and down regulate the nervous system. Evening breathing is a comprehensive baseline for all techniques.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Notice the current pace of the breath, regardless the state
Take a conscious breath to begin
Breathe in slowly for the count of 4 seconds or slow counting
Take a natural pause at the top of the inhale for approximately 1 second
Exhale to the count of 4 seconds or slow counting
Repeat this for 30 rounds or 2 minutes
If a 4 count patterns feels too short, long or not supportive increase or decrease to a count that creates calm and ease
Once completed, release the technique and return to a natural breathing pattern

techniques.title: 360 Breathing
techniques.text: This circumferential technique is a focused variation of belly/chest breathing that emphasises full torso breath capacity. Use 360 degree breathing to encourage a full breath and oxygen distribution and even inflation of rib cage expansion.
howTo: Find a comfortable seat
Place one hand on the belly and one hand on the chest
Breathe slowly and fully into both belly and chest equally, feeling both hands rise and expand
Focus on expanding evening in a 360 degree direction
Pause at the top of the breath for 1 second
Exhale slowly letting the belly and chest empty
Repeat this for 10 rounds or 60 seconds

techniques.title: Coherent breath
techniques.text: Coherent breathing is to breath at a rate of 5.5 breaths per minute, which generally translates into inhaling and exhaling to the count of 5.5 seconds. The purpose of this type of breathing is to increase heart rate variability.
howTo: Inhale into your belly for five seconds
Exhale for another five seconds
Repeat for three to five minutes. Please mind the following: Avoid breathing like a robot, or like you're scuba diving - breathe naturally as you usually do, just slower

techniques.title: 365 Method
techniques.text: 365 Method is a common technique often recommended by clinical professionals to counter accumulated stress. This method is a recommendation to breathe and practice repeat all 365 days of the year.
howTo: Using coherent breathing
Breathing practices three times a day
Breathing six times per minute (inhale for 5, exhale for 5)
Repeat for five minutes

techniques.title: CTS (Circle/Triangle/Square)
techniques.text:
Basic breathing exercise Optimize autonomic balance: a core breathing protocol
Establish a solid foundation for your breath with this foundational practice. Incorporating even breath, triangle breath, and box breath (remember this using the acronym 'circle, triangle, square' in reflection of the breathing patterns), this sequence promotes respiratory efficiency and overall balance. By syncing your inhalation and exhalation, you can cultivate a sense of evenness while optimizing oxygen intake.

techniques.title: 360° IPA
techniques.text:
Balanced breathing exercise Return to homeostatis: a sustained balance breathing protocol
This protocol is to help return the breath to balance or maintain a balanced homeostasis. These techniques help to regulate the autonomic nervous system, enhance oxygenation, and promote mental clarity. Balanced breathing reduces stress, improves focus, and cultivates overall equilibrium.

techniques.title: PXC
techniques.text:
Stress response breathing exercise Dissolve stress: a breath-based protocol
Respond to stress, anxiety, overwhelm or over stimulation with this rapid stress relief protocol and targeted breathing sequence. Combining the physiological sigh, extended exhale, and coherent breathing, this breathing protocol effectively activates the body's relaxation response by down regulating the sympathetic nervous system.

techniques.title: XLT
techniques.text:
Calming breathing exercise Sustained rest: a breath-based restoration protocol
Experience a scientifically-validated approach to stress management through this breathing practice. Combining extended exhale, low frequency humming breath and 4-7-8 breathing techniques, this protocol targets the body's stress response. By influencing the autonomic nervous system and promoting oxygenation, these exercises can induce a state a calming response and a parasympathetic activation.

techniques.title: E3J
techniques.text:
Energising breath exercise Ignite your energy: a breath- based boost protocol
Experience a surge of vitality with this energising breathing practice. By combining energising breath, three-part inhale and exhale breathing, and breath of joy, this sequence is designed to stimulate the body's energy systems. Increase oxygen intake, enhance focus, and elevate mood through the power of your breath.

techniques.title: 3CC
techniques.text:
Warming breath exercise Accelerate circulation: increase metabolic rate protocol
Using the breath to generate internal heat, this protocol incorporates cleansing and clearing breath performed at varying intensities to stimulate circulation, increase core temperature, and promote detoxification. By modulating the rhythm and pace of this technique, you can effectively target different circulatory channels, increase metabolic rate and stimulate your innate heating capacity.
NOTE: The three steps are the same technique (cleansing & clearing) performed at slow, medium, and fast pace respectively.

techniques.title: 3XV
techniques.text:
Sleep response breathing exercise. Deep rest: a sleep-enhancing breathwork protocol
Deep Rest is a scientifically validated breathwork protocol designed to enhance sleep quality and can significantly reduce physiological arousal, lower cortisol levels and promote relaxation. Optimize your body's natural sleep-wake cycle and experience improved sleep quality, leading to greater overall well-being.

techniques.title: CAP
techniques.text:
Relaxation breathing exercise Serenity: reduction of stimulus for optimal restoration
Excessive sensory stimulation can disrupt the body's natural relaxation response and hinder restorative processes. This breathwork protocol designed to minimize external stimuli, creating an optimal environment for relaxation and rejuvenation. By reducing sensory input, this practice can effectively promote a state of deep relaxation, lower stress hormones, and optimize the body's ability to repair and recover.
